citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: L
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: L

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: L

body:
Legally Obligated - Money the household is legally entitled to receive such as, but not limited to, wages, TFA , SSB , SSI , court ordered support or alimony, or contract payments; or

Money the household must legally pay to another person, household, or other entity such as, but not limited to, court-ordered child support, mortgage payments, or property taxes.

LIHEAP - Low-Income Heat and Energy Assistance Program

Lockout - A workplace closed by an employer to resist employee demands.

LUA - Limited Utility Allowance

Lump Sum - Money received one time and not expected to be received again.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: L 1
heading: L

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: L

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: M
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: M

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: M

body:
Mass Changes - Changes to eligibility or benefits on all SNAP cases, or a large number of cases, based on State or Federal government regulations or laws. The change is effective at the same time for all cases.

ME - Management Evaluation

Meal Delivery Services - A nonprofit meal delivery service authorized by FNS to provide meals to any member of an eligible EDG who is 60 years of age or older, or disabled, and unable to prepare their meals. The spouse of the aged or disabled EDG member may also purchase meals from the meal delivery service, regardless of age or disability.

Means-Tested Programs - Federal, State or local programs providing benefits to low-income individuals, such as, but not limited to:

Medicaid

Public Housing

Refugee Assistance

SAGA

SNAP

SSI

SSUP

TFA

Veteran Pensions

Mechanic's Lien - A security interest in the title to property for the benefit of those who supplied labor or materials that built, improved or repaired the property.

Medicaid - A program financed by State and Federal governments designed to provide health coverage to individuals who are unable to afford regular medical services.

Migrant Farmworker - An individual who temporarily leaves a principal place of residence in another state to come to Connecticut to accept seasonal employment in the planting, cultivating, raising, harvesting, handling, drying, packing, packaging, processing, freezing, grading or storing of any agricultural or horticultural commodity in its unmanufactured state. See also Seasonal Farmworker .

Monthly Entitlement - The amount of the benefit the EDG would receive if the IPV disqualified person was included in the EDG.

MyAccount - A personal account through ConneCT providing various DSS online services, such as:

Applications

Change reporting

Pre-screening for program eligibility

Renewals

Uploading documents and verifications
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: M 1
heading: M

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: M

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: N
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: N

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: N

body:
Naturalized Citizen - An individual who was not born a U.S. citizen, but who has become a U.S. citizen under the constitution and laws of the United States. Naturalized citizens have all the rights of a natural born citizen, except they cannot be president or vice-president of the U.S.

Net Income - The income after all allowable deductions are subtracted from the EDG’s gross income - for the net income test and calculating monthly benefits.

Non-Citizen - Any individual who is not classified as a United States citizen. Formerly known as “alien”.

Non-financial Eligibility - Application of rules to the characteristics of the individuals applying for or receiving SNAP benefits. These rules determine whether or not an individual is included in the group for which financial eligibility will be determined. EDG ineligibility is also possible for not meeting some of these rules. Non-financial factors include but are not limited to:

Citizenship

Employment and training requirements

Social security numbers

Student status

Non-immigrant - A non-citizen who seeks temporary entry to the U.S. for a specific purpose other than permanent residence. Non-immigrants maintain their permanent residence in another country. Most non-immigrants can be accompanied by a spouse and unmarried minor children.

Non-immigrants include, but are not limited to:

Exchange visitors

Foreign government officials

Individuals engaged to be married to U.S. citizens

International representatives

NATO officials

Non-citizens in transit through the U.S.

Religious workers

Students

Temporary workers and trainees

Treaty traders and investors

Visitors for business and for pleasure

Nonprofit - An organization not operating to make a profit.

Notice of Action (NOA) - A notice advising the EDG that benefits increased, decreased, did not change, or the case will close, after action is taken on their case.

The notice includes the following information:

The reason for the change (or that benefits did not change)

How the EDG’s benefit changed

The change effective date

Contact information for questions

The EDG’s right to a fair hearing

The EDG’s right to continued benefits (for decreases or closures)

The EDG is responsible for any overissuance if continued benefits are issued and the fair hearing is reversed.

If the notice is informing the EDG of a benefit decrease or closure, it is issued using either:

adequate notice , or

advance notice .

Notice of Expiration (NOE) - NOEs advise the EDG:

Their benefit period is ending

The due date to submit a timely-filed renewal application for uninterrupted benefits

The renewal application can be submitted:

in-office by the EDG or AR,

by mail,

online through MyAccount , or

through SSA for pure SSI EDGs.

Where to file the renewal application (office address and phone number)

Of their right to file a renewal application with only a:

signature,

legible name, and

address;

Of their interview requirements

Of their responsibility to rescheduled a missed interview

Of the results of failure to timely apply for renewal

Of their fair hearing rights

Notice of Missed Interview (NOMI) - The NOMI tells the EDG:

they did not complete their interview,

they must call to complete or schedule an interview or their application will be denied,

the days, work hours, and phone number to call the Benefit Center, and

the date by which the interview must be completed.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: N 1
heading: N

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: N

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: O
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: O

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: O

body:
OCSS - Office of Child Support Services

Offset - Reducing the amount of an overpayment by the amount of an underpayment .

OLCRAH - Office of Legal Counsel, Regulations, and Administrative Hearings

Opportunity to Receive Benefits - The EDG has an EBT card and benefits are authorized.

ORR - Office of Refugee Resettlement

Overpayment - The amount of benefits:

issued to an EDG exceeding the eligible amount, or

that were trafficked .
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: O 1
heading: O

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: O

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: P
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: P

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: P

body:
Parolee - An individual who is ineligible to enter the U.S. as a refugee, immigrant or non-immigrant who has been allowed entry into the U.S. by the DHS . This provision of the law is only used for emergency, humanitarian, and public interest reasons.

Partnership - A business organization in which 2 or more individuals join to manage and operate a business. Each owner contributes money, property, labor, or skills and expects to share in the profits or losses of the business.

Payment-In-Kind (PIK) Program - A program through the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), Farm Service Agency, created to take large acres out of production to address farm surpluses, without adding to government program costs. Farmers are paid in-kind from the crops held. Some crops held include but are not limited to:

Corn

Cotton

Cranberries

Grain Sorghum

Rice

Sugar Beets

Wheat

Periodic Report Form (PRF) - Form W-1054, Periodic Report Form (PRF), is sent to all EDGs with a 12 month cycle to report required change information at the 6th month.

Personal Effects - Belongings of individuals such as, but not limited to:

Books

Clothing

Grooming aids

Hobby equipment

Jewelry

Personally Identifiable Information (PII) - Personally Identifiable Information (PII) is information that can be used to distinguish or trace an individual’s identity, such as the first name or first initial and last name, along with one or more of the following types of data:

Date of birth

Driver’s license number

Financial records

Passport number

Social Security number

State identification number

Piecework - Work done by the piece and paid at a set rate per unit.

PIN - Personal Identification Number

Plan for Achieving Self-Support (PASS) - Program provided under the Social Security Act to help individuals receiving Supplemental Security Income (SSI) get a job or start a business.

POA - Power of Attorney

Post-secondary Education Institution - Any public or private educational institution (including correspondence or on-line institutions) which:

normally requires a high school diploma or equivalency certificate for enrollment; or

offers degree programs regardless of whether a high school diploma is required.

Examples: colleges, universities, business, vocational, trade, and technical schools.

An institution of higher education does not include any adult basic education program.

Pre-strike Income - All earned and unearned income normally received in a month when a person isn’t on strike .

Primary Wage Earner (PWE) - The primary wage earner is the EDG member with the most earned income in the 2 months prior to a work requirement violation . The earned income must be from a job of at least 20 hours or more per week, or equal to the federal minimum wage multiplied by 20 hours.

Prisoner Verification System (PVS) - A match to identify EDG members 18 years of age or older who are incarcerated.

Probation / Parole Violator - An individual who is in violation of conditions of probation or parole imposed by a State or federal law. Law enforcement must be actively seeking the individual to enforce these conditions.

Program Informational Activities - Activities to share information about SNAP through publications, interviews, posters, public contacts, etc.

Examples include, but are not limited to:

Nutrition Assistance Programs such as:

Connecticut Nutrition Assistance Program (CTNAP)

Food banks

The Emergency Food Assistance Program (TEFAP).

Nutritional information regarding menus, food preparation, food choices, etc.

Rights and responsibilities of SNAP applicants and recipients

Special Supplemental Food Program for Women, Infants, and Children (WIC) information.

Protected Health Information (PHI) - Protected Health Information is information that identifies and provides at least one piece of medical information about an individual, such as physical or mental conditions, medications, hospitalizations, etc.

Puerto Rico Nutrition Assistance Program (NAP) - A block grant nutritional assistance program operated in Puerto Rico.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: P 1
heading: P

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: P

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Q
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Q

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Q

body:
QC - Quality Control

QCR - Quality Control Review

Qualified Health Professional - An individual licensed by the Connecticut Medical Examining Board to provide medical care or other medical services. If questionable, go to https://www.elicense.ct.gov/Lookup/LicenseLookup.aspx to determine if a person is licensed in Connecticut.

Qualified Non-Citizen - An individual who is lawfully present in the United States who meets one of the following criteria:

Compacts of Free Association (COFA) citizen

Cuban Entrant

Haitian Entrant

Lawful Permanent Resident (LPR)

Qualified Tuition Programs - Plans operated by a state or educational institution allowing individuals to save funds for a specified beneficiary’s college or other post-secondary training costs.

Qualifying Work Quarter - The amount of earnings required for a quarter of coverage by Social Security. It is also called “Social Security Credit” or just “credit”. Certain non-citizens with 40 quarters of coverage are eligible non-citizens for SNAP.

Questionable Information - Information that is unclear, inconsistent, inaccurate, contradictory, or outdated (no longer reflects the household’s situation).
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Q 1
heading: Q

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Q

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: R
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: R

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: R

body:
RCE - Regular Categorical Eligibility

REAA - Refugee Education Assistance Act of 1980

Real Property - Real property is:

land, buildings, crops, or other assets attached to or within the land, or

improvements or fixtures permanently attached to the land or a structure on it.

Reasonable Accommodation - Reasonable accommodation means making reasonable changes to the Department’s policies, practices, or procedures to allow an individual with a disability to fully participate in the services, benefits, activities, and programs administered by the Department. Reasonable accommodation includes, but is not limited to:

Changes in the application or renewal process to allow an individual to establish and maintain eligibility.

Completing a home visit.

Providing materials in Braille, tape, or large print.

Requesting assistance of a specialized worker.

Reasonable accommodation does not include:

altering the nature of the program,

causing undue fiscal or administrative burden on the Department, or

waiving any eligibility factor.

Refugee - A non-citizen admitted to the United States under Section 207 of the Immigration and Nationality Act. Refugees are non-citizens who sought residence in the United States in order to avoid persecution in their country of origin. Unlike asylees , who did not apply for the right to lawfully stay in the United States until after their arrival, refugees apply for and are granted the right to lawfully enter the United States while living overseas.

Refusal to Cooperate - Refusal to cooperate means the household is able to cooperate, but shows the worker/QC reviewer they will not take required actions to complete the required process.

Example: The EDG refuses to be interviewed not just misses the interview.

Renewal - A complete review of all eligibility factors required to determine continued eligibility for SNAP benefits.

Representative Income - Representative income is amounts within the range of what would be the typical or usual amount of the EDG’s income.

Representative Payee - An individual who receives payment on behalf of another individual who cannot manage or direct the management of their benefits or payments.

Retail Food Store - Retail food store generally means an establishment authorized by FNS to accept benefits.

The following require authorization by FNS:

Communal dining facility

Farmers’ markets

Meal delivery service

Retail grocery/convenience stores

Wholesale food distributors

Note: This list is not all inclusive. FNS may authorize other types of establishments.

The following establishments may be authorized by FNS to accept benefits or may be authorized representatives/shoppers for the residents:

Group living for the disabled facilities

Homeless shelters

Residential Addiction treatment centers

Shelters for battered individuals

Retirement, Survivors, and Disability Insurance (RSDI) - Commonly referred to as Social Security Benefits; paid by the Social Security Administration. Verify RSDI using BENDEX .

Royalty Payment - A payment to an owner for the use of property.

Examples :

Copyrights

Franchises

Music

Natural resources

Patents

heading: R

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: R

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: S
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: S

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: S

body:
S-Corporation - A “S-Corporation” is a legal entity that is separate and distinct from its owners. Corporations are guided by a group of officers (board of directors). Corporations are owned by their stockholders, who share in the profits and losses generated through the firm’s operations. It confers a special tax status to shareholders and operates like a partnership. Income from an S-corporation is taxed at the individual level. The income is passed through to the shareholders based on each shareholder’s pro rata share.

Seasonal Farmworker - An individual who accepts seasonal employment in the planting, cultivating, raising, harvesting, handling, drying, packing, packaging, processing, freezing, grading or storing of any agricultural or horticultural commodity in its unmanufactured state. See also Migrant Farmworker .

Semi-monthly - Occurs twice a month.

Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP) - An employment-based job training program for older Americans providing training for low-income, unemployed seniors.

SFSNAP - State Funded Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program

Shared Households - Two or more households living together and sharing shelter expenses. The households do not have to share all shelter expenses for it to be a shared shelter situation. The households only need to indicate they live together and share a shelter expense. Individual expenses may be divided, households may pay designated expenses, or one household may pay a set amount toward any or all shelter expenses to another. However, the combined total of the shared portions cannot exceed the actual cost incurred for the housing unit.

Significant Return - The ownership interest in an asset, after deducting the cost to sell the asset and the amount owed on the asset, is more than $1,500.

SIV - Special Immigrant Visa

Social Security Benefits (SSB) - Common name for RSDI benefits paid by the SSA .

Social Security Number (SSN) - An identifying number established for an individual by the SSA . The number is used to:

prevent duplicate participation in State and Federal programs, and

to obtain income and asset information through computer matches.

Sponsor - An individual who signed an Affidavit of Support , Form I-864 , I-864A , or similar agreement on behalf of a non-citizen as a condition of the non-citizen’s entry or admission into the United States as a permanent resident.

Sponsored Non-Citizen - A non-citizen lawfully admitted for permanent residence into the U.S. under the condition they have an individual ( sponsor ) who signs an Affidavit of Support to guarantee financial support of the non-citizen.

SSA - Social Security Administration

Stable Income - Income received in a fixed amount from the same source on a regular schedule.

State Administered General Assistance (SAGA) - A cash assistance program for individuals who are unable to work due to a documented medical condition, or for certain other non-medical reasons.

State Data Exchange (SDX) - Verifies Supplemental Security Income (SSI) .

State Supplement Program (SSUP) - A cash assistance program for elderly or disabled individuals to help meet basic living costs.

State Verification Exchange System (SVES) - An on-line query with the Social Security Administration to verify:

40-qualifying work quarters information

Black Lung payment

Citizenship

Social Security income

Social Security numbers

Supplemental Security Income (SSI)

Strike - A refusal to work organized by a group of employees as a form of protest, usually in an attempt to gain concessions from their employer.

SUA - Standard Utility Allowance

SUA$ - Annual energy payments of $20.01 for households:

containing a member who is age 60+ and/or have a disabled member,

and

whose heat is included in their rent,

or

have no current heating/cooling obligation.

Receipt of the SUA$ qualifies the household to receive the SUA .

Summer EBT (S-EBT) - G ives assistance to children who qualify or would qualify for free or reduced priced meals (FRPM) through the National School Lunch Program (NSLP), but who are not receiving these meals due to summer break.

Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) - Authorized by the Food and Nutrition Act of 2008, as amended, assistance helps low income individuals and families stretch their food budget to put 3 healthy meals on the table every day.

Supplemental Security Income (SSI) - A Federal cash assistance program to help elderly or disabled individuals with little or no income meet basic needs.

Surrounding Property - Property the house is on and all other property, not separated by property owned by someone else. Surrounding property also includes property separated by a public road or other right-of-way.

Suspected Intentional Program Violation (SIPV) - Suspected IPV pending a final IPV determination. IPV is established by:

an administrative disqualification hearing ,

court adjudication,

the individual signing a disqualification consent agreement, or

the individual signing a waiver of their right to an administrative disqualification hearing on the official Agency waiver form.

Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) - The Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) system is a service that helps determine the immigration status of benefit applicants and their eligibility for SNAP benefits.

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: T
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: T

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: T

body:
Temporary Family Assistance (TFA) - A cash assistance program for basic and special needs paid to recipients of Jobs First.

Trafficked Benefits - Buying, exchanging or selling SNAP benefits for cash or non-food items, including but not limited to:

Ammunition

Certain controlled substances

Explosives

Firearms

Reselling a product purchased with SNAP benefits for non-food items or cash.

Returning a container for a deposit if the product was purchased with SNAP benefits when the contents were intentionally discarded.

Trafficking Victim - Trafficking victims are individuals who have experienced severe forms of trafficking:

Sex trafficking in which a commercial sex act is induced by force, fraud, or coercion, or in which the person induced to perform such act has not attained 18 years of age; or the recruitment, harboring, transportation, provision; or

Obtaining of a person for labor or services, through the use of force, fraud, or coercion for the purpose of subjection to involuntary servitude, peonage, debt bondage, or slavery.

Transitional Benefits Alternative (TBA) - The Transitional Benefits Alternative (TBA) provides benefits to EDGs leaving TFA . TBA ensures the EDG continues to meet their nutritional needs as they move from TFA to work. TBA guarantees the EDG a set benefit amount for 5 months.

Transitional Child Care (TCC) - Subsidies offered to families to help pay for child care after transitioning from cash assistance to employment.

TUA - Telephone Allowance

heading: T

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: U
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: U

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: U

body:
Underpayment - Benefits the EDG was entitled to, but were not received. Underpayments are only restored if the EDG did not cause them.

Unearned Income - Cash or contributions received by an individual for which they do not perform work or provide a service.

Unemployment Compensation Benefits (UCB) - Benefits paid by the Connecticut Department of Labor to an individual whose job ended due to lay-off, firing without good reason, or quitting with good reason.

Unfit for Work - Indicators of individuals being unfit for work include:

a drug or alcohol addict not in a rehabilitation program with an indicator of unfitness for work, such as but not limited to:

Fired from a job due to their addiction.

High/drunk during the interview.

chronically homeless . Chronically homeless individuals may not have access to upkeep of their personal hygiene or clothing, or appropriate clothing for an interview.

mentally or physically unable to work based on the worker’s observation;

mentally or physically unable to work as verified by a qualified health professional ; or

receiving any temporary or permanent disability benefits.

Uninterrupted Benefits - At renewal, an eligible EDG’s benefits must be available at its normal issuance time, provided all renewal actions are completed.

United States Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) - An agency in the Department of Homeland Security. USCIS is responsible for non-citizenship procedures and rules. Formerly called the Immigration and Naturalization Service (INA).

United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) - A Federal department made up of 29 agencies that support agriculture, economic development, food, natural resources, nutrition, and rural development.

Urban Indian - An individual is an “Urban Indian” per 25 U.S.C. 1603(18), if they:

Reside in an urban center and meet at least one of the following four criteria:

Regardless of if they live on or near a reservation, is a member of a tribe, band, or other organized group of Indians, including those tribes, bands, or groups terminated since 1940 and those recognized now or in the future by the State in which they reside, or who is a descendant, in the first or second degree of any such member;

Is an Eskimo or Aleut or other Alaska Native,

Is determined to be an Indian for any purpose under regulations promulgated by the Secretary of Interior; or

Is determined to be an Indian under regulations promulgated by the Secretary of Health and Human Services.

Urban center is defined as any community which has a sufficient urban Indian population with unmet health needs to warrant assistance under subchapter IV of the IHICA, as determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services.

body:
VA - Veteran’s Administration

Vehicle - Any motorized device that transports individuals or property.

Verified Upon Receipt - Verified upon receipt means the information is not questionable, and the provider is the primary source of the information. No further verification is required.

Voter Registration Coordinator (VRC) - A designated worker at DSS regional offices in charge of:

maintaining supplies of ED-671A/S, Mail-In Voter Registration Application Forms,

verifying completeness of the voter registration applications,

sending W-619, Receipt of Application, to individuals who submitted voter registration applications through DSS, and

forwarding voter registration applications to the appropriate town hall within 10 days of receipt.

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: W

body:
Withholding of Deportation - An order issued by an immigration judge allowing an individual to remain in the U.S. who shows more than a 50% chance they will be persecuted in their home country due to:

membership in a social group,

nationality,

political opinion, or

race.

Women, Infants, and Children (WIC) - The Special Supplemental Nutrition Program for Women, Infants, and Children (WIC) is a Federal assistance program through FNS . WIC provides services for health care and nutrition to low-income pregnant, breastfeeding, and non-breastfeeding postpartum women, and children up to age 5, who are at nutritional risk.

Workers' Compensation (WC) - A form of insurance paying a benefit to an employee who is injured on a job covered by workers’ compensation.

Workfare Programs - Activities with public or private non-profit employers where ABAWD s work off the value of the household’s monthly SNAP benefit as a condition of eligibility.

Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA) - Federal programs to prepare youth and unskilled adults for entry into the labor force. These programs also provide job training to economically disadvantaged individuals, and other individuals facing serious barriers to employment. WIOA amended the Workforce Investment act of 1988.

Workforce Partnerships - Programs operated by a private employer, an organization representing private employers, or a nonprofit organization providing services relating to workforce development; or an entity identified as an eligible provider of training services under Section 122(d) of WIOA. Workforce partnerships may include multi-State agency programs and must follow the Fair Labor Standards Act of 1938.

body: Age 6 7 CFR 273.7(b)(1)(iv) 7 CFR 273.5(b)(8) For households containing a child age 6 or under, the parent or other household member responsible for the care of the child must register for work if they are not eligible for another work registration exemption. If the household member is a student and responsible for the care of a dependent under the age of 6, they are an exempt student in higher education. Age 12 7 CFR 273.5(b)(9)-(10)(i-ii) For households containing a child between the ages of 6-12, and the parent or other household member responsible for the care of the child has inadequate child care, they are an exempt student in higher education. For a single parent enrolled in higher education full-time and responsible for the care of a dependent child under 12, they are an exempt student. Age 14 A parent or other household member responsible for the care of a dependent child under 14 years of age is exempt from the ABAWD time limits. Age 17 and under 7 CFR 273.9(c)(7) The earned income for any household member under the age of 18, who is in elementary or secondary school, is excluded. Age 18 or older 7 CFR 273.5(b) 7 CFR 273.7 7 CFR 273.24(c) Check for another student exemption; if none, household member is an ineligible student. Check for another work exemption; if none, household member must work register. Count earned income. Check for an ABAWD exemption; if none, household member is an ABAWD with time limits. Age 22 or older 7 CFR 273.1 If the household member purchases and prepares (P&P) separately, they can potentially be a separate household from parents. Age 50 or older 7 CFR 273.5(b)(1) Eligible student Age 60 or older 36-month approval if no earned income and all adult household members are age 60+ and/or disabled. Net income test only. $4,500 asset maximum when gross income is greater than 200%. Entitled to excess medical expense deduction. Not subject to the shelter CAP, unless ineligible. Age 65 or older 7 CFR 273.24(c) Exempt from the ABAWD Time limits.

body:
Use the following chart as a guide to determine the appropriate utility allowance for expedited service.

Household Utility Expense Utility Allowance Lives in public housing and pays excess utilities that include heating or cooling costs. SUA Lives with another household and pays or shares rent that includes an amount for utilities, or helps with the utility costs, that include heating or cooling costs. SUA Owns or is buying or renting their home and is billed for utilities including heating or cooling costs. SUA Receives a Direct Cash Benefit payment of at least $20.01 in the current month or previous 12 months, and the household includes a member who is 60+ and/or disabled. SUA Receives a SUA$ payment of at least $20.01 in the current month or previous 12 months, and the household includes a member who is 60+ and/or disabled. SUA Receives energy assistance through HUD, FmHA, or another program but incurs heating and cooling expenses that exceed the amount of the assistance, and the household includes a member who is 60+ and/or disabled. SUA Lives in public housing and pays for at least 2 utilities including excess costs that do not include heating or cooling costs. LUA Lives with another household and pays or shares rent that includes an amount for utilities, or helps with at least 2 utility costs, that do not include heating or cooling costs. LUA Receives energy assistance through HUD, FmHA or another program that covers all heating and cooling expenses but the household incurs at least 2 utility expenses that do not include heating/cooling. LUA Lives in public housing and only pays phone costs. TUA Lives with another household and pays or shares rent, and helps only with the phone bill. TUA Rents their residence and all utilities except the phone are included in the rent. The household pays for a phone. TUA Lives with another household and pays or shares rent, and all utilities are included. None

body:
Substantial lottery and gambling winnings is defined as a cash prize won in a single game, before taxes or other amounts are withheld, which is equal to or greater than the SNAP asset limit for elderly or disabled SNAP households with income above 200% of the FPL ($4,500 in FFY2025).

Receipt of lottery / gambling winnings that equal or exceed the threshold is considered a mandatory reporting requirement and households must report this change by the 10th day of the month following the month the change occurred (example: household member receives $5,000 from a single lotto ticket on 12/15/22. They must report receipt of these winnings to DSS by 1/10/23).

Staff should inform all households at initial grant and renewal of these mandatory reporting requirements during the SNAP interview. SNAP households who report receipt of lottery/gambling winnings that meet or exceed this threshold will be disqualified from SNAP.

Once reported, the lottery/gambling winnings must be considered a countable asset. Households are no longer considered “categorically eligible” and are subject to the 130% FPL income limit and asset limit.

body: Most Common IRS Forms What the Form is Used For Form 1040 US Individual Income Tax Return Summarizes all income reported to IRS. Do not use this form to determine income. It is only a summary of the income reported to IRS. Form 1065, U.S. Return of Partner Income Identifies income and expenses from a partnership. Schedule K-1, Partner’s Share of Income, Deductions, Credits, identifies the individual’s share from the partnership. Form 1099-R, Distributions Identifies payments for services, annuities, pensions, etc. Form 1120-S, US Income Tax Return for an S Corporation Identifies income and expenses from an S corporation. Schedule K-1, Partner’s Share of Income, Deductions, Credits, identifies the individual’s share from the corporation. Form 4562, Depreciation and Amortization Identifies deductions for depreciation and amortization, for vehicles and certain property. Form 4835, Farm Rental Income and Expenses Identifies income and expenses for farm rentals. Form 8829, Expenses for Business Use of Your Home Identifies office-in-the-home expenses. Line 7 of the 8829 identifies the business portion of the expense. Schedule C, Profit or Loss Identifies income and expenses for non-farm businesses. Schedule C-EZ, Net Profit from Business Simplified version of Schedule C Schedule D, Capital Gains and Losses Identifies capital gain income or losses. Schedule E, Supplemental Income and Loss Identifies income and expenses for rental business, partnerships, corporations, estates, trusts, and farm rental. Schedule F, Profit or Loss from Farming Identifies income and expenses for farm businesses.

This chart summarizes when verification is required and the action taken when verification is not provided.

Note: Most interim changes that result in decreased benefits are not processed. For exceptions, see Action on Interim Changes.

Element Expedited Application Non- Expedited Application Renewal Periodic Report and Interim Change ABAWD Countable Months (CT and out-of- state) Postpone verification* for expedited month(s); then follow non-expedited processing rules. Verify ABAWD CT countable months and 36-month time period, including out-of-state countable months and time periods if it appears the individual had countable months out-of-state. Use the individual’s statement if verification cannot be obtained from the other state. Verify ABAWD CT countable months and 36-month time period, including out-of-state countable months and time periods if it appears the individual had countable months out-of-state. Use the individual’s statement if verification cannot be obtained from the other state. Periodic Report - Verify new adult EDG members ABAWD CT countable months and 36-month time period, including out-of-state countable months and time periods if it appears the individual had countable months out-of-state. Use the individual’s statement if verification cannot be obtained. Interim Change – Verify new adult EDG members ABAWD CT countable months and 36-month time period, including out-of-state countable months and time periods if it appears the individual had countable months out-of-state. Use the individual’s statement if verification cannot be obtained. Decreases Benefits/Closes Case: See Note at the top of chart. Assets Postpone verification* for expedited month(s); then follow non-expedited processing rules. Verify countable assets only if the EDG is not categorically eligible. Deny the application if not verified by the due date. Verify countable assets only if the EDG is not categorically eligible and if: the previously verified asset amount has changed, newly obtained assets, or if questionable. Deny the renewal if not verified by the due date. Process without the expense if not verified by the due date. Verify legal obligation, amount paid and who it is paid to. Process without the expense if not verified by the due date. Continue with the previously verified amount if not verified by the W-1348 due date. Verify and act on the change at renewal. Decreases Benefits/Closes Case: Act on the reported change. Verify at renewal, if questionable, and verification was not provided with the PRF. Interim Change - Increases Benefits: Act on the change. Verify, if questionable. Disability Postpone verification* for expedited month(s); then follow non-expedited processing rules. Verify the disability determination. Do not code the individual as disabled if not verified by the due date. Verify only if it is a new report of disability. Do not code the individual as disabled if not verified by the due date. Periodic Report and Interim Change – Verify only if a new report of disability. Do not code the individual as disabled if not verified by the due date. Earned Income Postpone verification* for expedited month(s); then follow non-expedited processing rules. Verify. Deny the application if not verified by the due date. Verify if: the source changed, or the source has not changed but the amount changed by more than $50, if there is income from a new EDG member, or the amount is questionable (inaccurate, incomplete, inconsistent, or outdated). Deny the renewal if not verified by the due date. Periodic Report – Increases Benefits: Verify. Act on the change if verification is provided. Do not disqualify the individual until verification is received. Decreases Benefits/Closes Case: See Note at the top of chart. Verify: previously unreported expenses, total recurring medical expenses that changed by more than $25, or if questionable. Periodic Report and Interim Change – Verify only if benefits will increase and the expense is: previously unreported expenses, a recurring medical expense that changed by more than $25, or if questionable. Note: Do not act on expense changes: reported by someone other than the EDG, and that require contacting the EDG to verify. Non-citizen Status Postpone verification* for expedited month(s); then follow non-expedited processing rules. Verify. Treat the individual as an ineligible non-citizen if not verified by the due date. Verify if a change in non-citizen status is reported, or the status of a new non-citizen EDG member being added. Treat the individual as an ineligible non-citizen if not verified by the due date. Periodic Report – Verify if a change in non-citizen status is reported, or the status of a new non-citizen EDG member being added. Treat the individual as an ineligible non-citizen if not verified by the due date. Residency Postpone verification* for expedited month(s); then follow non-expedited processing rules. Verify. Deny the application if not verified by the due date. Verify if questionable. Deny the renewal if not verified by the due date. Periodic Report and Interim Change – Verify if questionable. Note: Get the new shelter amounts from the EDG. See Shelter and Utility Expenses, below. Shelter and Utility Expenses Postpone verification* for expedited month(s); then follow non-expedited processing rules. Verify if questionable. Process without the expense if not verified by the due date. Verify if questionable. Process without the expense if not verified by the due date. If questionable: Send the EDG a W-1348, Proofs We Need, requesting any required verifications not provided with the PRF. Allow the EDG 10 days to verify the reported change. If the address didn’t change and the EDG fails to provide verifications by the W-1348 due date, do not change the shelter/utility expenses. Verify and act on the change at renewal. If the address changed and the EDG fails to provide verifications by the W-1348 due date, do not allow any shelter/utility expenses. Decreases Benefits/Closes Case: Act on the reported change. Verify at renewal, if questionable , and verification was not provided with the PRF. Interim Change - Increases Benefits: Act on the change if not questionable. Verify, if questionable. If questionable: Send the EDG a W-1348, Proofs We Need, requesting any required verifications not provided with the PRF. Allow the EDG 10 days to verify the reported change. If the address didn’t change and the EDG fails to provide required verifications by the W-1348 due date, do not change the shelter/utility expenses. Verify and act on the change at renewal. If the address changed and the EDG fails to provide required verifications by the W-1348 due date, do not allow any shelter/utility expenses. Verify and act on the change at renewal. Decreases Benefits/Closes Case: See Note at top of chart. Social Security Number Postpone verification* for expedited month(s); then follow non-expedited processing rules. Verify through enumeration validation. The EDG must verify differences or information not validated. Deny the individual or application (for single individual EDGs) if not provided or applied for by the due date. Verify SSNs not previously provided, and for new members. citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets

body: The EDG has $900 in a savings account and $900 in a checking account. The EDG received $400 in wages during the current month deposited into the checking account. The $400 current income is excluded as an asset for the current month, leaving $1,400 in countable assets.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets 1
heading: Current Income

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets

body: Mr. and Mrs. A have 2 children. Mrs. A is disabled and has gross income greater than 200% of the FPL . Each child owns savings bonds. The cash value of the savings bonds is $1,000 for each child. One child is an ineligible student . The EDG has no other assets. Do not count the ineligible student’s $1,000 cash value. The EDG has $1,000 in countable assets.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets 2
heading: Non-Member Assets

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets

body: Ms. J and Ms. B have a jointly owned checking account with a $3,000 balance. Ms. J and Ms. B can each access the full amount of the account without the other’s approval. The entire balance is countable to both Ms. J and Ms. B.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets 3
heading: Jointly Owned Assets - Excluded Member Assets

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets

body: Mr. W jointly owns a piece of property with his 6 siblings. Mr. W cannot sell the property without his siblings’ approval. Not all the siblings are willing to sell the property. The property is not countable for Mr. W.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets 4
heading: Excluded jointly-owned assets

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets

body: Ms. A, age 56, is legally blind and lives alone. Ms. A added her daughter’s name to her checking account. Ms. F writes checks out of the account only to pay her mother’s expenses. Ms. F provides verification from her mother that the money in the account is only Ms. A’s money, and she only has her daughter’s name on the account to write checks to pay her bills. This account is not an asset to Ms. F, because the money belongs to Ms. A and the intent is that the money is used exclusively for Ms. A.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets 5
heading: Partial or No Ownership

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets

body: Mr. C. does not have enough money to pay his employees. Mr. C. takes out a loan for the purpose of paying his employees. The lender has a legal requirement placed on the loan that restricts the money from being used for purposes other than Mr. C. paying his employees’ wages. The loan is exempt as an asset.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets 6
heading: Loans

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets

body:
An EDG with $2,400 in a savings account transferred ownership of non-excluded property worth $2,350 to try to qualify for SNAP benefits. The EDG’s asset limit is $4,500.

Calculate the disqualification period as follows:

· $2,350 countable value of the transferred property

+

· $2,400 bank account = $4,750.

· $4,750 - $4,500 asset limit = $250 over the asset limit.

Disqualify the EDG for 6 months, based on being $250 over the asset limit. See Disqualification Period Table .
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets 7
heading: Transferred Assets

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Assets

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Budgeting for Ineligible Members
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Budgeting for Ineligible Members

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Budgeting for Ineligible Members

body: The EDG has 5 members. Three members are eligible for SNAP. One ineligible member has unearned income of $150 per month. Divide the total ineligible member’s unearned income by the total number of household members (eligible and ineligible members) $150 ÷ 5 = $30. Multiply $30 x 3 eligible members = $90. $90 is the eligible members’ prorated share of the ineligible member’s unearned income.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Budgeting for Ineligible Members 1
heading: Income

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Budgeting for Ineligible Members

body:
Example One : The EDG has 4 members. Three members are eligible for SNAP. The ineligible member pays $100 per month legally obligated child support . Divide the total ineligible member’s expense by the number of EDG members (eligible and ineligible members) $100 ÷ 4 = $25. Multiply $25 x 3 eligible members = $75. $75 is the eligible members’ prorated share of the ineligible member’s legally obligated child support expense.

Example 2 : The EDG has 2 members. One member is eligible for SNAP. One member is an elderly and an ineligible member. The ineligible elderly member has medical expenses greater than $35 and is responsible for the heat bill. Prorate the ineligible elderly member’s medical expenses and allow the full SUA .

Note: The EDG is subject to both the gross income limit and the excess shelter deduction maximum because the only elderly or disabled member is ineligible.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Budgeting for Ineligible Members 2
heading: Expenses

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

body: An ABAWD submits an online application and reports they have no income or liquid assets. Their benefits stopped 3 months prior for failure to meet ABAWD work requirements. If neither the EDG nor another source has provided any information to indicate the ABAWD is exempt from work requirements, or has met work requirements for 30-consecutive days, the ABAWD is not eligible for expedited service or expedited benefits. Enter case notes about the reason for denial of expedited service. Process as a regular application.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service 1
heading: ABAWD Doesn't Meet Expedited Service Eligibility

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

body:
An ABAWD submits an online application and reports they have no income or liquid assets. The ABAWD used their 3-countable months. Information was provided by the EDG or another source that the ABAWD has become exempt, or has met the work requirement for 30-consecutive days. Attempt to obtain as much verification as possible within the 7-day expedited service processing timeframe. Postpone verification that can’t be obtained and process the expedited service. Enter case notes about the information that was provided.

If the postponed verifications are not receive by the due date, and the ABAWD reapplies, all verification must be provided before further benefits are issued.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service 2
heading: ABAWD Meets Expedited Service

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

body:
A non-citizen submits an online application and reports they have no income or liquid assets. Their previous application was denied for failure to meet eligible non-citizen status. The non-citizen provides enough information that they meet eligible non-citizen status now, but didn’t provide verification. Attempt to obtain verification within the 7-day expedited service processing timeframe. Postpone verification that can’t be obtained, and process the expedited service. Enter case notes about the information that was provided.

Note: If the non-citizen doesn’t provide enough information to determine they are eligible based on unverified facts, treat as an ineligible non-citizen.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service 3
heading: EDG with Potential Ineligible Non-Citizen

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

body: The EDG submits its application and is screened eligible for expedited service. The EDG completes the minimum actions the same day. Process the application for expedited service so benefits can be accessed by the 7th day from the application file date.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service 4
heading: Minimum Actions Completed Same Day

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

body: The EDG drops off its application, but does not stay for the interview. The application is screened as eligible for expedited service. The worker attempts 2 cold calls, but does not complete the interview. Identity is not verified. The EDG is sent an On-Demand Interview notice advising the EDG to contact the Benefits Center to complete the interview within 7 days from the date of application, and any other required actions that must be completed. The EDG completes the interview and verifies identity on the 4th day. Process the application for expedited service so benefits can be accessed by the 7th day from the application file date.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service 5
heading: Minimum Actions Completed Within 7 Days

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

body: The EDG submits its application online. The application is screened as eligible for expedited service. The worker attempts 2 cold calls, but does not complete the interview. Identity is verified. The EDG is sent a W-0001N, Notice of Action, advising the EDG to contact the Benefits Center to complete the interview within 12 days from the date of application. Process the application for expedited service so benefits can be accessed by the 7th day from the application file date.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service 6
heading: Identity Verified but Interview Not Completed

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

body: The EDG mails in a complete application and qualifies for expedited service. The worker attempts 2 cold calls, but does not complete the interview. Identity is not verified. The EDG is sent an On-Demand Interview notice advising the EDG to contact the Benefit Center to complete the interview within 7 days, and any other required actions that must be completed. The interview is not completed and identity is not verified. The EDG is sent Notice of Missed Interview (NOMI). The EDG loses its entitlement to expedited service after the 7th day. The application is processed under the normal 30-day policy. Update the expedited screen in ImpaCT to reflect the 30 day status and add case notes.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service 7
heading: Identity and Interview Not Completed Within 7 Days

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

body: The EDG mails in an application without enough information to determine eligibility for expedited service. The worker attempts 2 cold calls, but does not complete the interview. The EDG is sent an On-Demand Interview notice advising the EDG to contact the Benefit Center to complete the interview within 12 days, and any other required actions that must be completed. The EDG contacts the Benefit Center and completes the interview. The worker determines the EDG is eligible for expedited service during the interview. Expedited service processing timeframes begin on the date of the interview (the date eligibility for expedited service is discovered). The EDG completes the minimum actions. Process the application for expedited service so benefits can be accessed by the 7th day from the date expedited service was discovered (the interview date). Update the expedited discovery date in ImpaCT.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service 8
heading: Eligibility for Expedited Service Discovered After Application File Date

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service

body:
Example 1: The EDG applies on May 8th and received their last paycheck from work the same day. The EDG is ineligible for SNAP for the month of application (May) due to excess income. The EDG has no income for the month of June and qualifies for expedited service. Issue so the June benefits can be accessed within:

7 days of the application file date, or

by the first day of the 2nd month, whichever is later.

In this scenario, June benefits must be able to be accessed on June 1st.

Example 2: The EDG applies on May 29th and received their last paycheck from work the same day. The EDG is ineligible for SNAP for the month of application (May). The EDG has no income for the month of June and qualifies for expedited service. Issue June benefits so benefits can be accessed within:

7 days of the application file date, or

by the first day of the 2nd month, whichever is later.

In this scenario, June benefits must be able to be accessed by June 5th.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Expedited Service 9
heading: Ineligible for Expedited Service in the Month of Application

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body: Application received 1/25. Applicant verifies new employment starting 1/13 with wages received 1/18 and 1/25. Budget the 1/18 and 1/25 wage for January. For February, budget the average weekly wage times 4.3.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 1
heading: Income Starts in Application Month

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body: Application received 1/15. The applicant was paid weekly. The final wage will be received on 1/20. The applicant has not found a new job, and will have zero wages for February. For January budget only the actual wages for 1/6, 1/13 and 1/20. Budget zero income for February unless the EDG reports a change.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 2
heading: Income Stops in Application Month

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body: Application received 4/14. Applicant lost their job on 4/1 with their last check received 4/4. They state they were told they were eligible for unemployment compensation benefits (UCB) but the amount and date of receipt is unknown. Budget the 4/4 wage as the only income. The UCB income is not budgeted because the receipt date and amount are unknown. Remove the wage amount for May benefits.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 3
heading: Income Date and Receipt Unknown in Application Month

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body: Application received 11/15. Applicant verified quarterly retirement payments of $2200, received the first of January, April, July, and October. Budget $733.33 for November ($2200/3) and the following months. Budget income over the period it is intended to cover for the application month, even if it is not received in the application month.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 4
heading: Income Received Less Often Than Monthly

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body: Income is paid on the first of the month except when the first falls on a weekend or holiday, then it is received on the previous workday. Count the income as received on the first day of the month it is intended.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 5
heading: Income for the Month Intended

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body: A member of the EDG is a teacher. The contract for teaching covers a full year from August through the following July and pays $18,000. The teacher actually works August through May. The income from teaching is intended to support the family for the year. Budget $1500 ($18,000/12) for each month.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 6
heading: Annual Income

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body: A member of the EDG works during the summer as a lifeguard. The contract covers the period of May through August and pays $2000 for the 4-month period. The income is not intended as the EDG’s annual income. Budget $500 ($2000/4) for the months of May, June, July and August. Remove the income for September.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 7
heading: Not Intended as Annual Income

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body:
Application received 1/20. The only income is weekly wages which fluctuate because of ongoing shift changes. Budget actual wages for 12/28, 1/4, 1/11 and 1/18 unless the amount is not representative of future income. If not representative, average the number of pays necessary to obtain representative monthly income. (This may require more than 30 days.)

Convert to a monthly amount:

Add total wages used

Divide by the number of pays used

Multiply by 4.3
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 8
heading: Income Fluctuates in Application Month

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body:
Periodic report received 8/28. Recipient started working on 6/1 and is paid weekly on Thursday. The amount of hours they work depends on how busy they are at work. Budget:

the last 30 days pays if representative of normal income, or

use a longer period if needed to arrive at a representative income amount.

Whichever method is used to determine the representative amount, convert to a monthly amount:

Total all wages

Divide by the number of pays used

Multiply by 4.3 to arrive at an average monthly income.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 9
heading: Periodic Report

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body:
On 5/16 the recipient reports starting work on 3/22 and is paid biweekly. Obtain wage amounts for the 30 days prior to 5/16. Budget 4/28 and 5/12 wages, if representative of normal income, or an average of the 4/14, 4/28 and 5/12 wages if a longer period is needed. The 3/31 wage is not used because it is a partial pay. Convert to monthly amount:

Add total wages used

Divide by the number of pays used

Multiply by 2.15
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 10
heading: Interim Change

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body:
Renewal application received 3/2. Recipient works as a substitute teacher and works when called. Substitutes are paid on Mondays when they work the prior week. For January and February, the recipient was paid 1/18; 2/1 and 2/29. No wages were received 1/4, 1/11, 1/25, 2/8, 2/15, or 2/22.

To determine the representative income:

Total the wages received

Divide by 9 pay periods (including the weeks they did not get paid)

Multiply by 4.3
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 11
heading: Intermittent Income

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body:
Renewal application received 2/12. Recipient verified wages for the last 30 days and stated the 1/25 wage was lower because they called in sick one day and weren’t paid for that day. They don’t anticipate missing days in the future. Budget the average of the 1/18, 2/1 and 2/8 wages if they are representative. Convert to a monthly amount:

Total the 3 wages

Divide by 3

Multiply by 4.3
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 12
heading: Renewal Application

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body:
Households must provide a full four weeks or a month’s worth of consecutive paystubs.

If a member of the household submits a series of paystubs that is missing pay coverage for two weeks or less, workers may use the year to date (YTD) calculation to yield the amount for the missing wages:

Missing Wages = Most Recent YTD - Most Recent Wages - Previous YTD

Example on how to calculate missing pay stub using YTD:

Pay Date Gross Income YTD Income 6/6 $287.15 $5,432.98 6/13 $312.15 $5,745.13 6/20 ? ? 6/27 $275.00 $6,330.63

A check is missing from pay date 6/20 period. Since we have the pay stub information from the week before as well as the pay stub information from the week after, we can calculate for the missing information.

First, take the most current YTD and subtract that same check’s gross income. ($6,330.63 - $275)

That sum now becomes your revised YTD income for the missing pay stub. From that, you must subtract the previous YTD income that you have from the prior check. ($6,055.63 - $5745.13)

When you do that math, you will determine the gross weekly amount of the missing check. ($310.50)
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 13
heading: Calculating Missing Paystubs Using YTD

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body: Use gross income before any deductions in the income determination calculation. The following wage stub shows differing gross wage amounts. Budget the gross wage amount prior to any pre-tax deductions. $2692 is budgeted.
citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income 14
heading: Pre-Tax Gross Wage

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Income

body: Occasionally wage stubs do not show the total gross income. Review wage stubs carefully to determine the correct gross wage. For this wage stub, regular wages, shift differentials and overtime have to be added together to determine gross wages. Budget $402.08 gross income if the extras are expected to continue. Do not include additional wages not expected to continue.

heading: More than One Gross Income Amount

citation_label: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Shared Household vs Roomers
heading: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Shared Household vs Roomers

metadata.title: Connecticut SNAP Policy Manual: Shared Household vs Roomers

body:
Stella and her children live with her brother Luke and his son. Luke is buying his home. Stella pays 1/3 of the mortgage, $100 toward the heat bill, the entire water bill and her own phone. (Shared Households)

Rita lives with Jill. Rita pays Jill $500 a month for her own room and use of the common areas of the apartment. (Roomer)

Laurel moved back home with her mother. Her mother cannot afford her home on her Social Security payment. Laurel pays all the utility bills. Mom pays the yearly taxes and insurance on the home. (Shared Households)

Matt moved back home with his father. His father charges him $450 for lodging. Matt has his own room and pays for his own phone. (Roomer)

Sue and her children live with her friend Jane and her children. Jane rents the apartment with no utilities included. Sue pays Jane half of the rent and half of all utilities. (Shared Households)

Pete moved back in with his parents. He lost his job and could not afford his apartment on UCB . Pete pays his parents half of the heat and electric bills to stay with them. (Shared Households)

A qualifying work quarter was earned for each calendar quarter an individual was paid $50 or more in wages (including Migrant and Seasonal Farmworker wages).

Four quarters were earned for each taxable year in which self-employment earnings were $400 or more.

A quarter was earned for each $100 (limited to 4) of migrant or seasonal farmworker wages for years 1955-1977.

No more than 4 quarters can be earned in each year.

See https://www.ssa.gov/OACT/COLA/QC.html for the increment amount for each year.
